description: Rules governing who tells the story and how: narrative authority, story structure, creative prompts, collaborative worldbuilding, and tone.

authority.description: Mechanics distributing the right to narrate, frame, and decide story truth.

focuses.shared_narration: Narrative control is distributed among all players, who take turns or share the right to describe outcomes and world details rather than deferring to a single GM.
focuses.gm_less_rotation: There is no permanent GM; facilitation duties rotate among players by scene, act, or trigger (Fiasco, Microscope, The Quiet Year).
focuses.scene_framing_rights: The right to open a scene — choosing its location, participants, and stakes — is an explicit, allocated power (Primetime Adventures scene framing).
focuses.fail_forward: Failed rolls never stall the story; they move it somewhere worse or more complicated instead of producing a simple 'nothing happens'.
focuses.success_at_cost_narration: Marginal results grant success with a narrated cost or complication attached, making mixed outcomes a codified narrative tier (PbtA 7-9 results).
focuses.fact_declaration: Players can spend a resource to declare new story facts — an old contact, a convenient detail — that become true on the spot (Fate point declarations).
focuses.narration_rights_bidding: Players bid or spend currency to seize control of narration or contest another's contribution (Universalis coins).
focuses.final_authority_veto: A designated veto or redirect power lets the table reject a narration and demand another approach (Archipelago's 'Try another way' ritual phrases).
focuses.gm_agenda_principles: The GM's narration is bound by written agendas and principles that constrain what they say and why (Apocalypse World's agenda, principles, and moves).
focuses.paint_the_scene_delegation: The facilitator formally hands descriptive questions to players — 'what does it look like when...' — making them co-authors of texture and detail (Agon's paint-the-scene prompts).

structure.description: Mechanics shaping story pacing: acts, scenes, and time jumps.

focuses.three_act_pacing: Play is divided into formal acts with distinct rules or expectations per act, driving a setup-confrontation-resolution arc (Fiasco's two acts plus Tilt, My Life with Master's endgame).
focuses.montage_scenes: Compressed montage rules resolve stretches of activity — training, travel, research — as one framed sequence with brief contributions from each player.
focuses.flashbacks: Play can jump backward in time to establish preparation or history, often at a resource cost, with the flashback's outcome bearing on the present (Blades in the Dark flashbacks).
focuses.epilogues: The rules mandate closing epilogue scenes where each player narrates their character's fate after the main action ends.
focuses.act_breaks: Codified breaks between acts trigger bookkeeping, escalation, or refreshed resources, marking structural seams in the session.
focuses.scene_economy_limits: Each player or the table gets a fixed budget of scenes per act, making scene selection itself a strategic, story-shaping choice (Fiasco's scene count).
focuses.interlude_scenes: Quiet character-focused interludes are a distinct scene type with their own rewards, deliberately alternating with high-tension play (The One Ring fellowship phases).
focuses.cliffhangers: Sessions or episodes are structured to end on codified cliffhangers, with mechanical rewards or carryover for leaving matters unresolved.
focuses.endgame_triggers: The story enters a defined final phase when a trigger condition is met, switching to endgame rules until conclusion (Ten Candles' last candle, My Life with Master's endgame threshold).

prompts.description: Mechanics that generate story content from random or structured stimuli.

focuses.oracle_tables: Yes/no and inspiration tables answer questions about the world when no GM decides, letting dice adjudicate the unknown (Ironsworn's Ask the Oracle, Mythic GM Emulator).
focuses.story_cubes: Rolled picture dice or drawn image tokens supply symbols that players must weave into the unfolding narrative (Rory's Story Cubes).
focuses.random_plot_twists: A random table or deck injects plot twists that override current expectations and force the story onto a new course.
focuses.mad_libs_prompts: Fill-in-the-blank sentence templates prompt players to complete structured statements that become story truth (Fiasco playset elements, The Quiet Year project prompts).
focuses.tarot_card_prompts: Tarot or purpose-built divinatory cards are drawn and interpreted to seed scenes, characters, or fates (Nova's tarot spreads, Carved from Brindlewood mysteries).
focuses.leading_questions: The rules pose pointed questions to specific players — 'who here has wronged you?' — whose answers immediately bind as fiction (PbtA playbook questions, The King is Dead-style prompts).

worldbuilding.description: Mechanics for collaboratively creating settings, histories, and communities.

focuses.collaborative_map_creation: The table draws and annotates a shared map during play, with additions following formal turns or prompts (The Quiet Year's map, Beyond the Wall village creation).
focuses.community_creation: The group builds its home community — its resources, troubles, and notables — through a structured procedure before or during play (Stonetop's steading, Forbidden Lands strongholds).
focuses.setting_questions: Setup poses structured questions about the setting whose answers customize the world before play begins.
focuses.microscope_history: Players build a setting's history nonlinearly, nesting periods, events, and scenes anywhere on the timeline without ownership of any part (Microscope).
focuses.setting_truths_selection: The table selects among pre-written alternative 'truths' for major setting questions, assembling a canonical world from options (Ironsworn's Your Truths).
focuses.pantheon_creation: Players collaboratively author the setting's gods or great powers, whose established portfolios then constrain and color play (Dawn of Worlds-style god-building).
focuses.collaborative_npc_creation: Recurring NPCs are created or elaborated jointly by the table, with players adding traits, wants, and secrets that become canon.

tone.description: Mechanics enforcing genre feel, mood, and register.

focuses.genre_emulation_rules: The system codifies its genre's logic — what kinds of events can happen, who can die, how stories must arc — so play reproduces the source fiction's conventions.
focuses.pulp_conventions: Rules enforce pulp tropes: cliffhanger escapes, mook-sweeping heroes, and villains who monologue rather than execute (Spirit of the Century, Feng Shui).
focuses.comedy_mechanics: Humor is mechanically rewarded or generated — bonuses for jokes, tables of absurd mishaps, or rules that engineer farce (Paranoia's clone deaths, Fiasco's disasters).
focuses.horror_atmosphere_rules: Procedures deliberately manufacture dread at the table — dimmed lights, whispered narration, forbidden dice — beyond what any stat tracks (Ten Candles' darkness rituals, Dread's tower table presence).
focuses.tone_dials: The table explicitly sets dials for grit, lethality, or darkness that change which rules modules apply (Forged in the Dark hack dials, gritty realism variants).
focuses.trope_invocation: Players spend resources to invoke named genre tropes as one-shot effects, bending events to formula because the genre demands it.
